# Bottom-pane composer, popups, and mention input  `stage-10.1.2`

This stage is the TUI’s bottom-edge interaction layer: it runs during the main event loop and owns everything the user types, selects, or confirms in the bottom pane, from the persistent chat composer to temporary popups and modal request flows. `mod.rs` hosts that pane, routes keystrokes to the active view, and composes the composer, footer, status surfaces, and stacked overlays through the shared `BottomPaneView` contract in `bottom_pane_view.rs`.

At its core, `chat_composer.rs` builds on `textarea.rs` and `textarea/vim.rs` to provide rich editing, with draft, footer, popup, and attachment state split into focused state modules. It integrates paste detection (`paste_burst.rs`), history recall and Ctrl+R search, slash-command parsing (`prompt_args.rs`, `slash_command.rs`, `slash_commands.rs`, `slash_input.rs`, `command_popup.rs`), and mention/file lookup via plugin inventory, live file search, and the unified `mentions_v2` popup. Older specialized popups like `skill_popup.rs` and `file_search_popup.rs` support that same inline completion role.

The rest of the stage supplies reusable bottom-pane UI machinery—`list_selection_view.rs`, `multi_select_picker.rs`, and the public `composer_input.rs` wrapper—and concrete modal flows for settings, skills, memories, hooks, feedback, custom prompts, app-link guidance, MCP elicitation, and request-user-input rendering.

## Files in this stage

### Bottom-pane host and view contract
These files define the bottom-pane host, its shared view interface, and the public wrapper that exposes composer behavior outside the chat UI.

### `tui/src/bottom_pane/bottom_pane_view.rs`

`orchestration` · `cross-cutting bottom-pane runtime`

This file is the interface layer for bottom-pane components. `ViewCompletion` is a tiny enum that distinguishes accepted versus cancelled completion, but the main content is the `BottomPaneView` trait, which extends `Renderable` and supplies a large set of optional hooks with conservative defaults. The trait covers direct key handling, completion state, child-view dismissal behavior, stable view identity for refreshes, selection/tab preservation, Ctrl-C handling, Escape routing preference, paste integration, time-based paste-burst flushing, pre-render ticking, and delayed frame scheduling. It also defines three request-consumption extension points: approval requests, generic tool user-input requests, and MCP server elicitation form requests. By default, these methods return the original request unchanged, signaling that the active view does not consume it. There is also a dismissal hook for app-server requests resolved elsewhere and a terminal-title urgency hook used to surface 'Action Required' state. The design is intentionally default-heavy: implementers only override the capabilities they need, while the bottom-pane host can treat all views uniformly through one trait object interface.

##### `BottomPaneView::handle_key_event`  (lines 22–22)

```
fn handle_key_event(&mut self, _key_event: KeyEvent)
```

**Purpose**: Optional key-event handler for an active bottom-pane view. The default implementation ignores all keys.

**Data flow**: Accepts a `KeyEvent` and performs no state changes or output by default.

**Call relations**: Called by the bottom-pane host whenever the active view receives keyboard input. Concrete views override it to implement their own interaction logic.

### `tui/src/bottom_pane/mod.rs`

`orchestration` · `cross-cutting`

This file is the central controller for the chat UI’s lower pane. `BottomPane` owns a persistent `ChatComposer` plus a `view_stack` of transient `BottomPaneView` overlays, so modal flows can temporarily replace the composer without losing draft state. It also tracks delayed approval requests, task-running status, inline status/footer widgets, pending-input previews, pending thread approvals, context-window metadata, and the active runtime keymap.

The core control flow is in `handle_key_event`, `handle_paste`, and `pre_draw_tick_at`. Input is routed to the topmost active view first; release events are ignored for views, Esc may be translated into `on_ctrl_c` unless the view explicitly prefers normal key handling, and completed views are popped with `pop_active_view_with_completion`, which can cascade dismissal of parent views marked `dismiss_after_child_accept`. Without an active view, keys go to the composer, except that the configured interrupt binding can be intercepted to trigger the status widget while a task is running and no popup/Vim-insert escape should consume the key.

Approval requests are intentionally delayed after recent typing: `record_composer_activity_at` updates an idle deadline, `approval_prompt_delay_remaining` computes the remaining delay, and `maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at` promotes queued approvals into an `ApprovalOverlay` only after the user has been idle long enough. Modal entry pauses the status timer and disables composer input for request/elicitation flows; `on_active_view_complete` restores both.

Rendering is assembled lazily by `as_renderable_with_composer_right_reserve`. If a modal view exists, it alone is rendered. Otherwise the pane composes status, unified-exec footer, pending-thread approvals, pending-input preview, spacer rows, and the composer into nested `FlexRenderable`s, with an optional `ChatComposerRightReserveRenderable` wrapper when the caller needs right-side width reserved. Numerous setter methods simply forward configuration or state into the composer/status helpers and request redraws, but the file’s important invariants are around view-stack ownership, redraw scheduling, and keeping modal/task/composer interactions consistent. The extensive tests in the same file lock down edge cases such as Ctrl+C routing, delayed approvals, Esc-versus-interrupt behavior, buried-view dismissal, and render snapshots for status/footer combinations.

#### Function details

##### `BottomPane::new`  (lines 255–299)

```
fn new(params: BottomPaneParams) -> Self
```

**Purpose**: Constructs the bottom pane, initializes the composer and all auxiliary UI state, and wires in the initial keymap and optional skill mentions.

**Data flow**: Consumes `BottomPaneParams`, creates a `ChatComposer` with focus, event sender, key support, placeholder, and paste-burst settings, installs the frame requester and default runtime keymap into the composer, seeds skill mentions, initializes empty view/deferred-request collections and helper widgets, and returns the fully populated `BottomPane`.

**Call relations**: This is the creation entry for the bottom-pane subsystem and is used broadly by production setup and tests. It establishes the owned composer and all state later mutated by the pane’s orchestration methods.

*Call graph*: calls 5 internal fn (new, new, new, new, defaults); called by 24 (composer_shown_after_denied_while_task_running, ctrl_c_cancels_history_search_without_clearing_draft_or_showing_quit_hint, ctrl_c_on_modal_consumes_without_showing_quit_hint, drain_pending_submission_state_clears_remote_image_urls, esc_interrupts_running_task_when_no_popup, esc_release_after_dismissing_agent_picker_does_not_interrupt_task, esc_routes_to_handle_key_event_when_requested, esc_with_agent_command_without_popup_does_not_interrupt_task, esc_with_skill_popup_does_not_interrupt_task, esc_with_slash_command_popup_does_not_interrupt_task (+14 more)); 2 external calls (new, new).

##### `BottomPane::set_keymap_bindings`  (lines 362–372)

```
fn set_keymap_bindings(&mut self, keymap: &RuntimeKeymap)
```

**Purpose**: Applies one runtime keymap snapshot across the composer and pane-owned helper widgets so all local surfaces stay synchronized.

**Data flow**: Clones the provided `RuntimeKeymap` into `self.keymap`, forwards it to `composer.set_keymap_bindings`, derives the primary interrupt binding, updates `pending_input_preview` and any active `status` widget with that binding, and requests a redraw.

**Call relations**: Used on config reload or interactive remap. It coordinates multiple owned surfaces rather than updating only the composer.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (request_redraw, set_keymap_bindings, set_interrupt_binding, primary_binding); 1 external calls (clone).

##### `BottomPane::toggle_vim_enabled`  (lines 457–461)

```
fn toggle_vim_enabled(&mut self) -> bool
```

##### `BottomPane::pop_active_view_with_completion`  (lines 495–516)

```
fn pop_active_view_with_completion(&mut self, completion: Option<ViewCompletion>)
```

**Purpose**: Removes the top view and applies completion semantics that may also dismiss or preserve parent views.

**Data flow**: Pops the top of `view_stack`. If the popped view existed and completion is `Accepted`, it repeatedly pops parent views whose `dismiss_after_child_accept()` flag is set; if completion is `Cancelled`, it clears that flag on the new top view; then it calls `on_view_stack_depth_decreased()`.

**Call relations**: Used after active-view completion in `handle_key_event` and `on_ctrl_c`. It centralizes stack-unwinding policy for nested modal flows.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (on_view_stack_depth_decreased); called by 2 (handle_key_event, on_ctrl_c).

##### `BottomPane::approval_prompt_delay_remaining`  (lines 524–531)

```
fn approval_prompt_delay_remaining(&self, now: Instant) -> Option<Duration>
```

**Purpose**: Computes how much longer approval prompts should stay delayed after recent composer activity.

**Data flow**: Reads `last_composer_activity_at`, adds `APPROVAL_PROMPT_TYPING_IDLE_DELAY`, compares that deadline to the provided `Instant`, filters out zero durations, and returns the remaining `Duration` if the idle delay has not yet elapsed.

**Call relations**: Used by delayed-approval scheduling logic in `record_composer_activity_at`, `maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at`, and `push_approval_request`.

*Call graph*: called by 3 (maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at, push_approval_request, record_composer_activity_at).


##### `BottomPane::record_composer_activity_at`  (lines 533–540)

```
fn record_composer_activity_at(&mut self, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Records a typing/editing timestamp and, when approvals are queued, schedules a redraw for the future idle deadline.

**Data flow**: Stores the provided `Instant` in `last_composer_activity_at`. If there are delayed approval requests and `approval_prompt_delay_remaining(now)` returns a duration, it requests a redraw at that future time.

**Call relations**: Called from `handle_key_event` and `handle_paste` when user activity should postpone approval modals.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (approval_prompt_delay_remaining, request_redraw_in); called by 2 (handle_key_event, handle_paste); 1 external calls (is_empty).


##### `BottomPane::maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at`  (lines 542–569)

```
fn maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at(&mut self, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Promotes queued approval requests into an approval modal once the user has been idle long enough and no other view is active.

**Data flow**: Reads delayed approvals, current view-stack emptiness, and idle-delay state. If approvals are queued but the idle delay remains, it schedules a redraw for that delay and returns. Otherwise it pops the oldest delayed request from the front, creates an `ApprovalOverlay`, drains remaining delayed requests from the back into the overlay queue to preserve FIFO display order, pauses the status timer, and pushes the modal view.

**Call relations**: Called during `pre_draw_tick_at` and immediately after queueing approvals in `push_approval_request`. It is the bridge from deferred approval state to an actual visible modal.

*Call graph*: calls 5 internal fn (approval_prompt_delay_remaining, pause_status_timer_for_modal, push_view, request_redraw_in, new); called by 2 (pre_draw_tick_at, push_approval_request); 5 external calls (new, is_empty, pop_back, pop_front, clone).


##### `BottomPane::handle_key_event`  (lines 572–664)

```
fn handle_key_event(&mut self, key_event: KeyEvent) -> InputResult
```

**Purpose**: Routes a key event either to the active modal view or to the composer, while enforcing special rules for Esc, Ctrl+C-like cancellation, task interruption, paste-burst redraws, and delayed approvals.

**Data flow**: Reads the current view-stack state, key event kind/code/modifiers, composer text, task-running flag, popup/Vim state, and status widget. With an active view, it ignores release events, may translate Esc into `view.on_ctrl_c()` unless the view prefers normal handling, forwards other keys to `view.handle_key_event`, pops completed views, schedules redraws for paste bursts, and returns `InputResult::None`. Without an active view, it detects `/agent` drafts, optionally intercepts the configured interrupt binding to call `status.interrupt()`, records whether the key counts as composer activity, forwards the event to `composer.handle_key_event`, records activity timestamps, requests redraws as needed, schedules paste-burst redraws, and returns the composer’s `InputResult`.

**Call relations**: This is the pane’s main input-routing function. It is called by the parent chat UI and coordinates composer behavior, modal behavior, interrupt handling, and redraw scheduling.

*Call graph*: calls 11 internal fn (composer_should_handle_vim_insert_escape, composer_text, pop_active_view_with_completion, record_composer_activity_at, request_redraw, request_redraw_in, handle_key_event, is_in_paste_burst, popup_active, recommended_paste_flush_delay (+1 more)); 2 external calls (now, matches!).


##### `BottomPane::on_ctrl_c`  (lines 675–700)

```
fn on_ctrl_c(&mut self) -> CancellationEvent
```

**Purpose**: Handles a Ctrl+C cancellation attempt locally, giving active views first refusal, then composer history search, then draft clearing.

**Data flow**: If a view is active, it calls `view.on_ctrl_c()`, checks completion, possibly pops the view, optionally shows the quit-shortcut hint, requests redraw, and returns `Handled` or `NotHandled`. Without a view, it first tries `composer.cancel_history_search()`, then returns `NotHandled` if the composer is empty, otherwise clears the composer via `clear_composer_for_ctrl_c`, shows the quit hint, requests redraw, and returns `Handled`.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level Ctrl+C routing. It complements `handle_key_event` by handling explicit cancellation semantics rather than generic key dispatch.

*Call graph*: calls 6 internal fn (clear_composer_for_ctrl_c, composer_is_empty, pop_active_view_with_completion, request_redraw, show_quit_shortcut_hint, ctrl); 3 external calls (Char, matches!, cancel_history_search).


##### `BottomPane::handle_paste`  (lines 702–723)

```
fn handle_paste(&mut self, pasted: String)
```

**Purpose**: Routes pasted text to the active view or composer and tears down modal flows that complete as a result of paste handling.

**Data flow**: Reads whether the pasted string is empty. If a view is active, it forwards the paste to `view.handle_paste`, checks `view.is_complete()`, clears the entire view stack and calls `on_active_view_complete()` when complete, and requests redraw when needed. Otherwise it forwards to `composer.handle_paste`, records composer activity for non-empty pastes, and requests redraw when needed.

**Call relations**: Used by the parent UI for paste events. It mirrors `handle_key_event` but with paste-specific completion behavior.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (on_active_view_complete, record_composer_activity_at, request_redraw, handle_paste); 1 external calls (now).

##### `BottomPane::set_task_running`  (lines 1001–1026)

```
fn set_task_running(&mut self, running: bool)
```

**Purpose**: Transitions the pane into or out of task-running mode, creating or hiding the status widget and synchronizing interrupt hints.

**Data flow**: Reads the new `running` flag, stores it in `is_task_running`, forwards it to `composer.set_task_running`, and branches. On transition into running, it lazily creates `StatusIndicatorWidget` if absent, enables its interrupt hint and binding, syncs unified-exec summary text into the status row, and requests redraw. On transition out of running, it hides the status indicator.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level task lifecycle code such as submission/review flows. It coordinates composer state, status widget lifetime, and inline summary synchronization.

*Call graph*: calls 6 internal fn (hide_status_indicator, request_redraw, sync_status_inline_message, set_task_running, primary_binding, new); called by 2 (enter_review_mode_with_hint, submit_op); 2 external calls (clone, clone).

##### `BottomPane::apply_standard_popup_hint`  (lines 1088–1102)

```
fn apply_standard_popup_hint(&self, params: &mut list_selection_view::SelectionViewParams)
```

**Purpose**: Normalizes selection-view footer hints so cancelable popups get the pane’s standard hint line and non-cancelable ones do not show a misleading cancel hint.

**Data flow**: Mutably inspects `SelectionViewParams`. If `allow_cancel` is false and the footer hint is absent or equal to the standard popup hint, it clears the hint. If cancel is allowed and the hint is absent or equal to the standard popup hint, it replaces it with `self.standard_popup_hint_line()`.

**Call relations**: Called before constructing selection views in all selection-view creation/replacement paths.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (standard_popup_hint_line, standard_popup_hint_line); called by 4 (replace_active_views_with_selection_view, replace_selection_view_if_active, replace_selection_view_if_present, show_selection_view).


**Purpose**: Replaces the currently active selection view when its `view_id` matches the requested id.

```
        &mut self,
        view_id: &'static str,

    ) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Replaces the newest matching selection view anywhere in the stack without disturbing views above it.

**Data flow**: Searches `view_stack` from the end for a matching `view_id`; if none is found, returns `false`. Otherwise it notes whether the replaced view is active, applies standard popup hints, overwrites that stack slot with a new `ListSelectionView`, schedules the active view frame if the replaced view was on top, requests redraw, and returns `true`.

**Call relations**: Used when buried selection views need refreshing while preserving stacked modal context above them.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (apply_standard_popup_hint, request_redraw, schedule_active_view_frame, new); 2 external calls (new, clone).

##### `BottomPane::push_approval_request`  (lines 1349–1384)

```
fn push_approval_request(&mut self, request: ApprovalRequest, features: &Features)
```

**Purpose**: Handles a new approval request by either letting the active view consume it, delaying it after recent typing, or immediately showing an approval modal.

**Data flow**: Consumes an `ApprovalRequest` and `Features`. If a top view exists, it first offers the request to `view.try_consume_approval_request`; consumed requests stop there after a redraw. Otherwise it checks current idle-delay state: if approvals are already delayed or recent typing still blocks display, it pushes a `DelayedApprovalRequest` into `delayed_approval_requests` and calls `maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at(now)`; if not delayed, it constructs an `ApprovalOverlay`, pauses the status timer, and pushes the modal.

**Call relations**: Called when the agent requests approval. It coordinates active-view interception, delayed-approval UX, and modal creation.

*Call graph*: calls 6 internal fn (approval_prompt_delay_remaining, maybe_show_delayed_approval_requests_at, pause_status_timer_for_modal, push_view, request_redraw, new); 6 external calls (new, now, is_empty, push_back, clone, clone).


##### `BottomPane::push_user_input_request`  (lines 1387–1414)

```
fn push_user_input_request(&mut self, request: ToolRequestUserInputParams)
```

**Purpose**: Shows a request-user-input modal unless the active view consumes the request first.

**Data flow**: Consumes `ToolRequestUserInputParams`. If a top view exists, it first offers the request to `view.try_consume_user_input_request`; consumed requests stop after a redraw. Otherwise it constructs `RequestUserInputOverlay::new_with_keymap`, pauses the status timer, disables composer input with a contextual placeholder, and pushes the modal view.

**Call relations**: Called when the agent requests structured user input. It parallels approval handling but without delayed display.

*Call graph*: calls 5 internal fn (pause_status_timer_for_modal, push_view, request_redraw, set_composer_input_enabled, new_with_keymap); 3 external calls (new, clone, clone).


##### `BottomPane::push_mcp_server_elicitation_request`  (lines 1416–1501)

```
fn push_mcp_server_elicitation_request(
        &mut self,
        request: McpServerElicitationFormRequest,
    )
```

**Purpose**: Shows either an app-link suggestion modal or an MCP server elicitation modal for a server/tool request, unless the active view consumes it.

**Data flow**: Consumes an `McpServerElicitationFormRequest`. It first offers the request to the active view via `try_consume_mcp_server_elicitation_request`; consumed requests stop after redraw. Otherwise it checks `request.tool_suggestion()`: when a suggestion with `install_url` exists, it builds an `AppLinkView` with derived suggestion metadata, pauses the status timer, disables composer input with a suggestion-specific placeholder, and pushes that view. If no such suggestion exists, it builds `McpServerElicitationOverlay::new_with_keymap`, pauses the status timer, disables composer input with an MCP-specific placeholder, and pushes that modal.

**Call relations**: Called when the agent needs MCP-related user action. It branches between two different modal types based on request contents.

*Call graph*: calls 10 internal fn (pause_status_timer_for_modal, push_view, request_redraw, set_composer_input_enabled, new_with_keymap, request_id, server_name, thread_id, tool_suggestion, new_with_keymap); 4 external calls (new, matches!, clone, unreachable!).


##### `BottomPane::dismiss_app_server_request`  (lines 1503–1540)

```
fn dismiss_app_server_request(
        &mut self,
        request: &ResolvedAppServerRequest,
    ) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Dismisses any delayed approvals or stacked views associated with a resolved app-server request.

**Data flow**: Reads the target `ResolvedAppServerRequest`, removes matching delayed approvals with `retain`, then scans the view stack from top to bottom calling `view.dismiss_app_server_request(request)`. It records indices of views that became complete, removes them, calls `on_view_stack_depth_decreased()`, requests redraw if anything changed, and returns whether any delayed request or view was dismissed.

**Call relations**: Used when an app-server request is resolved elsewhere and any corresponding pending UI should disappear, including buried views.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (on_view_stack_depth_decreased, request_redraw); called by 1 (dismiss_app_server_request); 3 external calls (new, len, retain).

```
fn request_redraw(&self)
```


**Purpose**: Builds the pane’s renderable composition with no extra right-side reserve for the composer.

**Call relations**: Used by the `Renderable` trait methods as the default composition path.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (as_renderable_with_composer_right_reserve); called by 4 (cursor_pos, cursor_style, desired_height, render).


##### `BottomPane::as_renderable_with_composer_right_reserve`  (lines 1656–1712)

```
fn as_renderable_with_composer_right_reserve(
        &'_ self,
        composer_right_reserve: u16,
    ) -> RenderableItem<'_>
```

**Purpose**: Builds the render tree for the pane, choosing either the active modal view or a composed stack of status/footer/preview surfaces plus the composer, optionally wrapped to reserve right-side width.

**Data flow**: Reads active-view state, status/footer emptiness, pending preview state, and the requested reserve width. If an active view exists, it returns that view as a borrowed renderable. Otherwise it constructs nested `FlexRenderable`s containing the status widget, unified-exec footer when no status row exists, spacer rows between surfaces, pending-thread approvals, pending-input preview, and finally either the composer directly or a `ChatComposerRightReserveRenderable` wrapper when reserve width is nonzero.

**Call relations**: This is the pane’s rendering composition hub. It is called by all render/height/cursor methods and determines whether the composer or a modal view owns the visible area.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (active_view, is_empty, is_empty, new); called by 5 (as_renderable, cursor_pos_with_composer_right_reserve, cursor_style_with_composer_right_reserve, desired_height_with_composer_right_reserve, render_with_composer_right_reserve); 3 external calls (new, Borrowed, Owned).

##### `tests::approval_request_is_delayed_after_recent_typing`  (lines 2073–2095)


### `tui/src/public_widgets/composer_input.rs`

`orchestration` · `request handling`

This file adapts the internal `bottom_pane::ChatComposer` into a reusable public widget named `ComposerInput`. The wrapper owns a `ChatComposer` plus a private unbounded Tokio channel pair used to satisfy the composer's requirement for an `AppEventSender`. The sender is intentionally retained in `_tx` only to keep the channel alive; the receiver is drained after every mutating operation so internal events do not accumulate when this widget is used outside the main app event loop.

Construction is opinionated: `ComposerInput::new` always creates the composer with input focus enabled, paste-burst handling enabled, and `enhanced_keys_supported = true`, which preserves Shift+Enter newline behavior and the corresponding footer hint. The placeholder text is fixed to `Compose new task`.

Most methods are thin delegations to `ChatComposer`: emptiness checks, clearing text, paste handling, footer hint overrides, desired height, cursor position, rendering, and paste-burst status/flush timing. The main behavioral adaptation is `input`, which converts the composer's richer `InputResult` into the public `ComposerAction` enum. Only `InputResult::Submitted { text, .. }` escapes as `ComposerAction::Submitted(text)`; all other outcomes collapse to `ComposerAction::None`.

The wrapper is therefore intentionally lossy but stable: callers get a simple text-input component with multiline editing, paste heuristics, and submit semantics, while internal app events remain fully encapsulated.

#### Function details

##### `ComposerInput::new`  (lines 36–48)

```
fn new() -> Self
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a public composer widget backed by an internal `ChatComposer` and a private app-event channel.

**Data flow**: It creates an unbounded Tokio channel, wraps the sender in `AppEventSender`, constructs `ChatComposer::new` with focus enabled, enhanced keys enabled, placeholder `"Compose new task"`, and paste-burst support enabled, then stores the composer, sender, and receiver in `ComposerInput`. It returns the initialized wrapper.

**Call relations**: This is the primary constructor used directly by callers and indirectly by `ComposerInput::default`. It wires the internal composer to a throwaway event channel because the public wrapper does not expose app-event handling.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (new, new); called by 2 (new, composer_input_renders_typed_characters); 1 external calls (unbounded_channel).


##### `ComposerInput::is_empty`  (lines 51–53)

```
fn is_empty(&self) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Reports whether the wrapped composer currently contains any text.

**Data flow**: It reads `self.inner` and returns the boolean result of `ChatComposer::is_empty()`. No state is changed.

**Call relations**: This is a direct pass-through query on the internal composer.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_empty).


##### `ComposerInput::clear`  (lines 56–59)

```
fn clear(&mut self)
```

**Purpose**: Removes all current text and resets the composer's content to empty.

**Data flow**: It mutably accesses `self.inner` and calls `set_text_content(String::new(), Vec::new(), Vec::new())`, replacing the current text and associated metadata collections with empty values. It returns no value.

**Call relations**: This method is a direct mutation wrapper over the internal composer state; unlike input/paste methods it does not need to drain events because it does not route through event-producing handlers.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (set_text_content); 2 external calls (new, new).


##### `ComposerInput::input`  (lines 62–69)

```
fn input(&mut self, key: KeyEvent) -> ComposerAction
```

**Purpose**: Feeds a keyboard event into the composer and converts the result into the public `ComposerAction` abstraction.

**Data flow**: It takes a `KeyEvent`, passes it to `self.inner.handle_key_event(key)`, inspects the first tuple element, maps `InputResult::Submitted { text, .. }` to `ComposerAction::Submitted(text)` and all other variants to `ComposerAction::None`, then calls `drain_app_events()` before returning the action.

**Call relations**: This is the main interactive entrypoint for host code. It delegates editing and submit detection to `ChatComposer::handle_key_event`, then consumes any internal `AppEvent`s so the wrapper remains self-contained.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (handle_key_event, drain_app_events); 1 external calls (Submitted).


##### `ComposerInput::handle_paste`  (lines 71–75)

```
fn handle_paste(&mut self, pasted: String) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Passes pasted text through the composer's paste heuristics and reports whether it was handled.

**Data flow**: It takes an owned pasted `String`, calls `self.inner.handle_paste(pasted)`, stores the returned boolean, drains queued app events, and returns that boolean to the caller.

**Call relations**: This method is used when host code receives paste input separately from key events. Like `input`, it delegates behavior to `ChatComposer` and then clears any generated internal events.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (handle_paste, drain_app_events).


##### `ComposerInput::set_hint_items`  (lines 79–85)

**Call relations**: This is a customization hook for embedding contexts that want different footer hints while still using the same composer behavior.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (set_footer_hint_override).


##### `ComposerInput::clear_hint_items`  (lines 88–90)

##### `ComposerInput::drain_app_events`  (lines 126–128)

```
fn drain_app_events(&mut self)
```

**Purpose**: Discards any `AppEvent`s emitted by the internal composer so the public wrapper does not accumulate unread channel messages.

**Data flow**: It repeatedly calls `self.rx.try_recv()` in a loop until the channel is empty or disconnected, ignoring all received event payloads. It returns no value.

**Call relations**: This private helper is invoked after `input`, `handle_paste`, and `flush_paste_burst_if_due` because those internal composer operations may emit app events that have no meaning to public wrapper consumers.

*Call graph*: called by 3 (flush_paste_burst_if_due, handle_paste, input); 1 external calls (try_recv).

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (new).


### Composer text engine and input state
This group builds the reusable text-editing foundation and the low-level state pieces that the chat composer uses to manage drafts, paste behavior, attachments, and footer/popup presentation.

### `tui/src/bottom_pane/paste_burst.rs`

`domain_logic` · `cross-cutting`

This file encapsulates paste-burst detection in `PasteBurst`, a small state machine driven by timestamps rather than direct UI mutation. Its state combines `last_plain_char_time` and `consecutive_plain_char_burst` for burst classification, `pending_first_char` for briefly holding the first ASCII character to avoid rendering flicker, `buffer` plus `active` for accumulated paste content, and `burst_window_until` for a short post-burst Enter-suppression window. The constants define the heuristic: at least three closely spaced plain chars indicate a burst, with separate thresholds for inter-character grouping and active-buffer idle flushing.

The API is intentionally split by caller behavior. `on_plain_char` may retain the first ASCII char, begin buffering from that pending char, retroactively request buffering of already-inserted text, or append to an active burst. `on_plain_char_no_hold` skips the hold path for IME/non-ASCII input. Once buffering is active, callers append chars or newlines into `buffer`; later `flush_if_due` emits either a single typed char, a pasted string, or nothing depending on elapsed time and state. Retro-capture support is handled by `decide_begin_buffer`, which converts a character count into a UTF-8 byte start index with `retro_start_index` and only activates when the grabbed prefix looks paste-like (contains whitespace or is long). Separate methods distinguish flushing buffered content before unrelated input from merely clearing the classification window after non-char keys.

#### Function details

##### `PasteBurst::recommended_flush_delay`  (lines 208–210)

```
fn recommended_flush_delay() -> Duration
```

**Purpose**: Returns a delay slightly longer than the plain-character grouping threshold so tests or UI timers can reliably force a pending first character to flush. It encodes the module's intentional `>` timeout semantics.

**Data flow**: Reads the constant `PASTE_BURST_CHAR_INTERVAL`, adds 1 millisecond, and returns the resulting `Duration`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests and external scheduling code that need to cross the pending-char timeout deterministically.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (recommended_paste_flush_delay, ascii_first_char_is_held_then_flushes_as_typed); 1 external calls (from_millis).


##### `PasteBurst::recommended_active_flush_delay`  (lines 213–215)

```
fn recommended_active_flush_delay() -> Duration
```

**Purpose**: Returns a delay slightly longer than the active-buffer idle timeout so tests can reliably flush buffered paste content. It is test-only support for the active burst path.

**Data flow**: Reads `PASTE_BURST_ACTIVE_IDLE_TIMEOUT`, adds 1 millisecond, and returns the resulting `Duration`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need deterministic paste-buffer flushing after burst activity.

*Call graph*: called by 6 (ascii_burst_treats_enter_as_newline, burst_paste_fast_large_inserts_placeholder_on_flush, burst_paste_fast_small_buffers_and_flushes_on_stop, flush_after_paste_burst, ascii_two_fast_chars_start_buffer_from_pending_and_flush_as_paste, newline_suppression_window_outlives_buffer_flush); 1 external calls (from_millis).


##### `PasteBurst::on_plain_char`  (lines 218–248)

```
fn on_plain_char(&mut self, ch: char, now: Instant) -> CharDecision
```

**Purpose**: Classifies an incoming plain ASCII character as held typing, burst start, burst append, or retro-capture trigger based on timing and current state. It is the main entrypoint for the flicker-suppressed ASCII path.

**Data flow**: Takes `ch` and `now`, first updates timing counters via `note_plain_char`. If `active` is already true, it extends `burst_window_until` and returns `BufferAppend`. Otherwise, if a `pending_first_char` exists and arrived within `PASTE_BURST_CHAR_INTERVAL`, it activates buffering, clears the pending char, pushes the held char into `buffer`, extends the Enter-suppression window, and returns `BeginBufferFromPending`. If the consecutive burst count has reached `PASTE_BURST_MIN_CHARS`, it returns `BeginBuffer { retro_chars: consecutive - 1 }`. Otherwise it stores `(ch, now)` in `pending_first_char` and returns `RetainFirstChar`.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level input handling for plain ASCII chars; callers then interpret the returned `CharDecision` to decide whether to render, retro-grab, or append.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (note_plain_char); 1 external calls (duration_since).


##### `PasteBurst::on_plain_char_no_hold`  (lines 256–271)

```
fn on_plain_char_no_hold(&mut self, now: Instant) -> Option<CharDecision>
```

**Purpose**: Classifies a plain non-ASCII or IME character stream without ever holding the first character. It preserves responsiveness for input methods where delayed rendering would feel broken.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, updates timing via `note_plain_char`, and then either returns `Some(BufferAppend)` if already active, `Some(BeginBuffer { retro_chars: consecutive - 1 })` if the burst threshold has been reached, or `None` if the char should be treated as normal typing.

**Call relations**: Used by external key handling on non-ASCII paths instead of `on_plain_char`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (note_plain_char); called by 1 (handle_key_event_at).


##### `PasteBurst::note_plain_char`  (lines 273–282)

```
fn note_plain_char(&mut self, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Updates the burst timing heuristic for one newly observed plain character. It either increments the current burst count or resets it when the gap is too large.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, reads `last_plain_char_time`, compares elapsed time against `PASTE_BURST_CHAR_INTERVAL`, increments `consecutive_plain_char_burst` with saturation when still inside the interval, otherwise resets it to 1, and stores `now` into `last_plain_char_time`.

**Call relations**: Internal helper shared by both plain-char classification entrypoints.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (on_plain_char, on_plain_char_no_hold); 1 external calls (duration_since).


##### `PasteBurst::flush_if_due`  (lines 293–317)

```
fn flush_if_due(&mut self, now: Instant) -> FlushResult
```

**Purpose**: Emits buffered state once the relevant timeout has elapsed, producing either a pasted string, a delayed single typed char, or nothing. It is the periodic drain point for the state machine.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, chooses the timeout based on `is_active_internal()` (`PASTE_BURST_ACTIVE_IDLE_TIMEOUT` for active buffer, otherwise `PASTE_BURST_CHAR_INTERVAL`), checks whether `last_plain_char_time` is older than that timeout, and then: if timed out with active/internal burst state, clears `active`, takes `buffer`, and returns `FlushResult::Paste`; if timed out without active buffer but with a pending first char, clears `pending_first_char` and returns `FlushResult::Typed(ch)`; otherwise returns `FlushResult::None`.

**Call relations**: Called by periodic UI ticks or equivalent scheduling so buffered burst state eventually becomes concrete input.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_active_internal); 3 external calls (take, Paste, Typed).


##### `PasteBurst::append_newline_if_active`  (lines 324–332)

```
fn append_newline_if_active(&mut self, now: Instant) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Captures Enter as a newline inside an ongoing burst instead of letting the caller submit input. It extends the suppression window whenever newline is absorbed.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, checks `is_active()`, and if true appends `\n` to `buffer`, updates `burst_window_until`, and returns `true`; otherwise returns `false` without mutation.

**Call relations**: Used by callers that want Enter to join an in-progress burst payload.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_active).


##### `PasteBurst::newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit`  (lines 335–338)

```
fn newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit(&self, now: Instant) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Reports whether Enter should currently behave like newline because the system is in burst context or still inside the short post-burst suppression window. It separates newline-vs-submit policy from actual buffer mutation.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, computes whether `burst_window_until` still covers `now`, checks `is_active()`, and returns true if either condition holds.

**Call relations**: Called by `direct_insert_newline_should_insert` and by external Enter handlers that need burst-aware behavior.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_active); called by 1 (direct_insert_newline_should_insert).


##### `PasteBurst::direct_insert_newline_should_insert`  (lines 341–346)

```
fn direct_insert_newline_should_insert(&self, now: Instant) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Extends newline suppression to direct-insert callers that do not buffer chars first. It treats very recent plain-char activity as enough evidence to insert newline rather than submit.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, returns true if `newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit(now)` is true or if `last_plain_char_time` exists and is within `PASTE_BURST_CHAR_INTERVAL` of `now`.

**Call relations**: Used by external key handling paths that insert characters immediately instead of following the full buffering contract.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit); called by 1 (handle_key_event_at).


##### `PasteBurst::extend_window`  (lines 349–351)

```
fn extend_window(&mut self, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Refreshes the Enter-suppression window without changing buffer contents. It lets callers keep multiline paste behavior alive across related events.

**Data flow**: Takes `now` and sets `burst_window_until` to `now + PASTE_ENTER_SUPPRESS_WINDOW`.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level input handling when burst-like activity should keep Enter in newline mode.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (handle_key_event_at).


##### `PasteBurst::begin_with_retro_grabbed`  (lines 354–360)

```
fn begin_with_retro_grabbed(&mut self, grabbed: String, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Starts active buffering using text that the caller already removed from the UI via retro-capture. It seeds the burst buffer and suppression window in one step.

**Data flow**: Takes a `grabbed` string and `now`; if `grabbed` is non-empty it appends it to `buffer`, sets `active = true`, and updates `burst_window_until`.

**Call relations**: Used only by `decide_begin_buffer` after it decides a retro-grabbed prefix looks paste-like.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (decide_begin_buffer).


##### `PasteBurst::append_char_to_buffer`  (lines 363–366)

```
fn append_char_to_buffer(&mut self, ch: char, now: Instant)
```

**Purpose**: Appends one character to the burst buffer and refreshes the Enter-suppression window. It is the primitive write operation for active burst accumulation.

**Data flow**: Takes `ch` and `now`, pushes `ch` onto `buffer`, and sets `burst_window_until` to `now + PASTE_ENTER_SUPPRESS_WINDOW`.

**Call relations**: Called by external code after `CharDecision` indicates buffering, and internally by `try_append_char_if_active`.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (try_append_char_if_active).


##### `PasteBurst::try_append_char_if_active`  (lines 371–378)

```
fn try_append_char_if_active(&mut self, ch: char, now: Instant) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Conditionally appends a character only when the state machine is already in burst context. It lets callers opportunistically capture chars without rechecking state themselves.

**Data flow**: Takes `ch` and `now`, checks whether `active` is true or `buffer` is non-empty, and if so delegates to `append_char_to_buffer` and returns `true`; otherwise returns `false`.

**Call relations**: A convenience wrapper for callers that already suspect burst context but want a boolean success result.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (append_char_to_buffer).


##### `PasteBurst::decide_begin_buffer`  (lines 391–411)

```
fn decide_begin_buffer(
        &mut self,
        now: Instant,
        before: &str,
        retro_chars: usize,
    ) -> Option<RetroGrab>
```

**Purpose**: Determines whether already-inserted recent text should be retroactively reclassified as paste and moved into the burst buffer. It only triggers for prefixes that look sufficiently paste-like.

**Data flow**: Takes `now`, the text slice `before`, and a character count `retro_chars`. It computes `start_byte` with `retro_start_index`, clones `before[start_byte..]` into `grabbed`, classifies that text as paste-like if it contains whitespace or has at least 16 characters, and if so calls `begin_with_retro_grabbed(grabbed.clone(), now)` and returns `Some(RetroGrab { start_byte, grabbed })`; otherwise returns `None`.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level input handling after `BeginBuffer { retro_chars }` decisions to decide whether to retro-grab from the textarea.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (begin_with_retro_grabbed, retro_start_index).


##### `PasteBurst::flush_before_modified_input`  (lines 414–424)

```
fn flush_before_modified_input(&mut self) -> Option<String>
```

**Purpose**: Immediately emits buffered burst content before unrelated modified or non-char input is processed. It prevents transient burst state from leaking across semantic input boundaries.

**Data flow**: Checks `is_active()`. If false, returns `None`. Otherwise it clears `active`, takes `buffer` into a local string, appends any `pending_first_char` to that output, clears the pending char, and returns `Some(out)`.

**Call relations**: Used by callers before arrows, Ctrl/Alt-modified keys, and similar inputs that should not join the current burst.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_active); 1 external calls (take).


##### `PasteBurst::clear_window_after_non_char`  (lines 430–436)

```
fn clear_window_after_non_char(&mut self)
```

**Purpose**: Resets only the timing/classification window after non-char input. It assumes any buffered content has already been flushed elsewhere.

**Data flow**: Clears `consecutive_plain_char_burst`, `last_plain_char_time`, `burst_window_until`, `active`, and `pending_first_char`.

**Call relations**: Called after non-char input so subsequent typing starts a fresh classification window.


##### `PasteBurst::is_active`  (lines 441–443)

```
fn is_active(&self) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Reports whether the detector is in any transient burst-related state, including a held first char. It is broader than active buffering alone.

**Data flow**: Returns true if `is_active_internal()` is true or `pending_first_char` is `Some`.

**Call relations**: Used by newline handling and flush-before-modified-input logic to decide whether burst semantics are currently in effect.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_active_internal); called by 3 (append_newline_if_active, flush_before_modified_input, newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit).


##### `PasteBurst::is_active_internal`  (lines 445–447)

```
fn is_active_internal(&self) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Reports whether there is active or buffered burst content, excluding the held-first-char state. It is the narrower internal notion used for timeout selection.

**Data flow**: Returns `self.active || !self.buffer.is_empty()`.

**Call relations**: Used internally by `flush_if_due` and `is_active`.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (flush_if_due, is_active).


##### `PasteBurst::clear_after_explicit_paste`  (lines 449–456)

```
fn clear_after_explicit_paste(&mut self)
```

**Purpose**: Fully resets burst state after the caller has already handled an explicit paste event. It prevents stale timing or buffered content from affecting later typing.

**Data flow**: Clears `last_plain_char_time`, resets `consecutive_plain_char_burst` to 0, clears `burst_window_until`, sets `active = false`, empties `buffer`, and clears `pending_first_char`.

**Call relations**: Called by higher-level paste handling paths after explicit paste insertion.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (handle_key_event_at, handle_paste).


##### `retro_start_index`  (lines 459–469)

```
fn retro_start_index(before: &str, retro_chars: usize) -> usize
```

**Purpose**: Converts a count of trailing characters to a UTF-8 byte index into a string slice. It is the low-level helper that makes retro-capture safe on multibyte text.

**Data flow**: Takes `before` and `retro_chars`. If `retro_chars == 0`, returns `before.len()`. Otherwise it walks `before.char_indices()` in reverse, selects the nth trailing character boundary, returns its byte index if found, and falls back to `0` when the requested count exceeds the string length.

**Call relations**: Used by `decide_begin_buffer` to turn character-based retro-grab requests into byte slicing boundaries.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (decide_begin_buffer).


##### `tests::ascii_first_char_is_held_then_flushes_as_typed`  (lines 479–490)

```
fn ascii_first_char_is_held_then_flushes_as_typed()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that a single fast ASCII char is temporarily held and later emitted as ordinary typing if no burst follows. This protects the flicker-suppression path.

**Data flow**: Creates a default `PasteBurst`, feeds one char at `t0`, advances time past `recommended_flush_delay`, flushes, and asserts that the result is `Typed('a')` and the detector is no longer active.

**Call relations**: Exercises `on_plain_char`, `flush_if_due`, and `is_active` together on the simplest ASCII path.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (recommended_flush_delay); 4 external calls (from_millis, now, assert!, default).


##### `tests::ascii_two_fast_chars_start_buffer_from_pending_and_flush_as_paste`  (lines 495–515)

```
fn ascii_two_fast_chars_start_buffer_from_pending_and_flush_as_paste()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that two closely spaced ASCII chars transition from held-first-char state into active buffering and eventually flush as one pasted string. It validates the no-retro-grab fast path.

**Data flow**: Creates a detector, feeds `a` then `b` 1ms later, asserts `BeginBufferFromPending`, appends `b` into the buffer, advances time past `recommended_active_flush_delay`, flushes, and asserts the emitted paste string is `ab`.

**Call relations**: Covers the transition from `RetainFirstChar` to `BeginBufferFromPending` and then to `FlushResult::Paste`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (recommended_active_flush_delay); 4 external calls (from_millis, now, assert!, default).


##### `tests::flush_before_modified_input_includes_pending_first_char`  (lines 520–530)

```
fn flush_before_modified_input_includes_pending_first_char()
```

**Purpose**: Ensures that flushing before modified input includes a held first ASCII char even when no active buffer exists yet. This prevents pending state from being lost across unrelated keys.

**Data flow**: Creates a detector, feeds one ASCII char to enter pending-first-char state, calls `flush_before_modified_input`, and asserts the returned string is `a` and the detector is inactive afterward.

**Call relations**: Directly validates the modified-input flush path rather than timeout-based flushing.

*Call graph*: 4 external calls (now, assert!, assert_eq!, default).


##### `tests::decide_begin_buffer_only_triggers_for_pastey_prefixes`  (lines 535–552)

```
fn decide_begin_buffer_only_triggers_for_pastey_prefixes()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that retro-capture only activates for prefixes that look like pasted text, not short ordinary words. It protects against false positives on IME or rapid typing.

**Data flow**: Creates a detector, calls `decide_begin_buffer` first on `ab` and then on `a b`, asserts the first returns `None`, the second returns a `RetroGrab` starting at byte 1 with grabbed text ` b`, and confirms active state after the second call.

**Call relations**: Exercises `decide_begin_buffer`, `retro_start_index`, and the paste-like heuristic.

*Call graph*: 4 external calls (now, assert!, assert_eq!, default).


##### `tests::newline_suppression_window_outlives_buffer_flush`  (lines 557–579)

```
fn newline_suppression_window_outlives_buffer_flush()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that Enter remains treated as newline briefly after a burst has already flushed. This preserves multiline paste behavior when Enter arrives slightly late.

**Data flow**: Builds a burst from two fast chars, flushes it after the active timeout, asserts the detector is no longer active, then checks `newline_should_insert_instead_of_submit` both inside and after the suppression window.

**Call relations**: Validates the distinction between active buffering and the longer-lived `burst_window_until` policy window.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (recommended_active_flush_delay); 4 external calls (from_millis, now, assert!, default).


### `tui/src/bottom_pane/textarea/vim.rs`

`domain_logic` · `vim normal-mode command handling`

This file is the Vim text-object companion to `textarea.rs`. It defines the modal enums used by the textarea’s Vim state machine: `VimMode`, `VimOperator`, `VimPending`, `VimMotion`, `VimTextObjectScope`, and `VimTextObject`. The behavioral code lives in `impl TextArea`, where key events are translated into text-object scopes (`inner` vs `around`) and concrete objects (`word`, `big word`, paired delimiters, and quotes).

Word objects are computed from non-whitespace runs. `big_word_range_at_cursor` treats each contiguous non-whitespace run as one object, while `small_word_range_at_cursor` further splits runs with `split_word_pieces` so punctuation-separated pieces behave like Vim’s small words. Around-word expansion prefers trailing whitespace when present, otherwise leading whitespace, matching common Vim expectations for `aw`.

Delimiter objects scan the whole buffer with nesting support and choose the smallest enclosing pair containing the cursor, while skipping positions inside atomic textarea elements. Quote objects are intentionally line-local, ignore escaped quote characters, and also skip atomic elements. Both inner and around variants are supported, with `idx_range` handling inclusive quote ranges. The result is a set of precise byte ranges that the main textarea code can feed into delete/yank/change operators without duplicating parsing logic.

#### Function details

##### `TextArea::vim_text_object_scope_for_event`  (lines 64–83)

```
fn vim_text_object_scope_for_event(
        &self,
        event: KeyEvent,
    ) -> Option<VimTextObjectScope>
```

**Purpose**: Maps a key event to the Vim text-object scope selector `inner` or `around`.

**Data flow**: Checks the operator keymap bindings for `select_inner_text_object` and `select_around_text_object`, returning `Some(VimTextObjectScope::Inner)` or `Some(...::Around)` when matched, otherwise `None`.

**Call relations**: Pending operator handling calls this after `d`, `y`, or `c` to decide whether the next key should be interpreted as a text object.


##### `TextArea::vim_text_object_for_event`  (lines 85–111)

```
fn vim_text_object_for_event(&self, event: KeyEvent) -> Option<VimTextObject>
```

**Purpose**: Maps a key event to a concrete Vim text object such as word, big word, parentheses, brackets, braces, or quotes.

**Data flow**: Checks the configured text-object keymap fields in order and returns the corresponding `VimTextObject` variant or `None` if no binding matches.

**Call relations**: Once a scope like `i` or `a` has been recognized, pending text-object handling uses this to resolve the final object key.


##### `TextArea::text_object_range`  (lines 113–128)

```
fn text_object_range(
        &self,
        object: VimTextObject,
        scope: VimTextObjectScope,
    ) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Dispatches text-object range computation based on the selected object type and scope.

**Data flow**: Matches on `object` and delegates to `word_text_object_range`, `paired_text_object_range`, or `quoted_text_object_range`, passing through the requested `scope`. It returns an optional byte `Range<usize>`.

**Call relations**: The main textarea’s Vim text-object handler calls this before applying delete/yank/change to the resulting range.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (paired_text_object_range, quoted_text_object_range, word_text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::word_text_object_range`  (lines 130–144)

```
fn word_text_object_range(
        &self,
        scope: VimTextObjectScope,
        big_word: bool,
    ) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Computes the range for small-word or big-word text objects and expands it for `around` scope when requested.

**Data flow**: Chooses either `big_word_range_at_cursor()` or `small_word_range_at_cursor()` to get the inner range, then returns it unchanged for `Inner` or passes it to `expand_word_around` for `Around`.

**Call relations**: This is the shared implementation behind `iw`, `aw`, `iW`, and `aW`.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (big_word_range_at_cursor, expand_word_around, small_word_range_at_cursor); called by 1 (text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::big_word_range_at_cursor`  (lines 146–150)

```
fn big_word_range_at_cursor(&self) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Finds the contiguous non-whitespace run under the cursor for Vim big-word text objects.

**Data flow**: Builds all non-whitespace runs with `non_ws_runs()`, then returns the first run where the cursor overlaps the range or sits exactly at its end.

**Call relations**: Used by `word_text_object_range` when the selected object is `BigWord`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (non_ws_runs); called by 1 (word_text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::small_word_range_at_cursor`  (lines 152–171)

```
fn small_word_range_at_cursor(&self) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Finds the punctuation-aware small-word piece under the cursor, or the last piece when the cursor is at the end of a run.

**Data flow**: Iterates non-whitespace runs, skips runs the cursor neither overlaps nor ends, splits the matching run with `split_word_pieces`, returns the first piece containing the cursor, otherwise remembers the last piece and returns it when the cursor is at run end, falling back to the whole run if needed.

**Call relations**: Used by `word_text_object_range` for ordinary Vim word objects that should split on punctuation.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (cursor_is_at_range_end, cursor_overlaps_range, non_ws_runs); called by 1 (word_text_object_range); 1 external calls (split_word_pieces).


##### `TextArea::non_ws_runs`  (lines 173–189)

```
fn non_ws_runs(&self) -> Vec<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Collects all contiguous non-whitespace ranges in the buffer.

**Data flow**: Scans `self.text.char_indices()`, starts a run when entering non-whitespace, closes and pushes it when whitespace is encountered, and pushes any trailing run at EOF. Returns `Vec<Range<usize>>`.

**Call relations**: Both big-word and small-word text-object selection build on this run inventory.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (big_word_range_at_cursor, small_word_range_at_cursor); 1 external calls (new).


##### `TextArea::cursor_overlaps_range`  (lines 191–193)

```
fn cursor_overlaps_range(&self, range: &Range<usize>) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Checks whether the cursor lies inside a half-open byte range.

**Data flow**: Returns `range.start <= cursor_pos && cursor_pos < range.end`.

**Call relations**: Small-word selection uses this to decide whether the cursor is currently inside a candidate run or piece.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (small_word_range_at_cursor).


##### `TextArea::cursor_is_at_range_end`  (lines 195–197)

```
fn cursor_is_at_range_end(&self, range: &Range<usize>) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Checks whether the cursor is exactly at the end boundary of a non-empty range.

**Data flow**: Returns true when `range.start < range.end` and `cursor_pos == range.end`.

**Call relations**: Word text-object logic uses this so text objects still resolve when the cursor sits just after a word.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (small_word_range_at_cursor).


##### `TextArea::expand_word_around`  (lines 199–205)

```
fn expand_word_around(&self, inner: Range<usize>) -> Range<usize>
```

**Purpose**: Expands an inner word range to an around-word range by including adjacent whitespace.

**Data flow**: Computes trailing whitespace with `following_whitespace_end(inner.end)`; if any exists, returns `inner.start..following`. Otherwise it includes preceding whitespace by returning `preceding_whitespace_start(inner.start)..inner.end`.

**Call relations**: Used only for `aw`/`aW` semantics after an inner word range has been found.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (following_whitespace_end, preceding_whitespace_start); called by 1 (word_text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::following_whitespace_end`  (lines 207–216)

```
fn following_whitespace_end(&self, start: usize) -> usize
```

**Purpose**: Finds the end of the whitespace run immediately following a given position.

**Data flow**: Scans forward from `start` over `self.text[start..].char_indices()`, extending `end` while characters are whitespace, and returns the final byte index.

**Call relations**: Around-word expansion prefers trailing whitespace and uses this helper to measure it.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (expand_word_around).


##### `TextArea::preceding_whitespace_start`  (lines 218–227)

```
fn preceding_whitespace_start(&self, end: usize) -> usize
```

**Purpose**: Finds the start of the whitespace run immediately preceding a given position.

**Data flow**: Scans backward over `self.text[..end].char_indices().rev()`, moving `start` left while characters are whitespace, and returns the resulting byte index.

**Call relations**: Around-word expansion falls back to this when there is no trailing whitespace to include.

##### `TextArea::paired_text_object_range`  (lines 229–264)

```
fn paired_text_object_range(
        &self,
        scope: VimTextObjectScope,
        open: char,
        close: char,
    ) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Finds the smallest enclosing paired-delimiter range containing the cursor, with support for nested pairs and inner/around scope.

**Data flow**: Scans `self.text.char_indices()` while skipping positions inside atomic elements, pushes opening-delimiter indices onto a stack, pops on matching closing delimiters, and whenever the resulting pair encloses the cursor computes either the inner range `(open+len)..close` or around range `open..close_end`. It keeps the shortest valid candidate and returns it.

**Call relations**: `text_object_range` delegates here for parentheses, brackets, and braces.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_inside_element); called by 1 (text_object_range); 1 external calls (new).


##### `TextArea::quoted_text_object_range`  (lines 266–298)

```
fn quoted_text_object_range(
        &self,
        scope: VimTextObjectScope,
        quote: char,
    ) -> Option<Range<usize>>
```

**Purpose**: Finds the smallest enclosing quoted range on the current line, ignoring escaped quotes and atomic elements.

**Data flow**: Restricts scanning to `beginning_of_current_line()..end_of_current_line()`, toggles between open and close quote positions for the requested quote character, skips positions inside elements or escaped by backslashes, and when a quote pair encloses the cursor computes either the inner contents or the inclusive around range via `idx_range`. It returns the shortest valid candidate.

**Call relations**: `text_object_range` delegates here for double quotes, single quotes, and backticks.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (is_escaped, is_inside_element, idx_range); called by 1 (text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::is_inside_element`  (lines 300–304)

```
fn is_inside_element(&self, pos: usize) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Checks whether a byte position lies inside any atomic textarea element.

**Data flow**: Iterates `self.elements` and returns true if `pos >= start && pos < end` for any element.

**Call relations**: Delimiter and quote text-object scanners use this to ignore placeholder contents when matching structural characters.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (paired_text_object_range, quoted_text_object_range).


##### `TextArea::is_escaped`  (lines 306–315)

```
fn is_escaped(&self, pos: usize) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Determines whether a quote at a given position is escaped by an odd number of preceding backslashes.

**Data flow**: Walks backward over characters before `pos`, counts consecutive trailing `\` characters, stops at the first non-backslash, and returns whether the count is odd.

**Call relations**: Quote text-object scanning uses this to avoid treating escaped quotes as delimiters.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (quoted_text_object_range).


##### `idx_range`  (lines 318–320)

```
fn idx_range(open_idx: usize, close_idx: usize, quote: char) -> Range<usize>
```

**Purpose**: Builds an inclusive around-quote range from opening and closing quote indices.

**Data flow**: Returns `open_idx..close_idx + quote.len_utf8()` so the closing quote is included in the range.

**Call relations**: Quoted text-object range computation uses this helper for `around` scope.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (quoted_text_object_range).


### `tui/src/bottom_pane/textarea.rs`

`domain_logic` · `interactive text input and composer rendering`

This file contains the full editor model for the bottom-pane composer. `TextArea` owns the UTF-8 text buffer, byte-position cursor, cached wrapped line ranges, preferred visual column for vertical movement, atomic `TextElement` ranges, a single kill buffer with `Characterwise` vs `Linewise` semantics, and cloned runtime/editor/Vim keymaps. The design explicitly preserves the kill buffer across whole-buffer replacement so higher-level flows can clear or restore drafts without losing the most recent yank target.

Editing is element-aware and grapheme-aware. Cursor movement and deletion use `prev_atomic_boundary`/`next_atomic_boundary`, which treat placeholder elements as indivisible spans and otherwise step by Unicode grapheme boundaries. Range edits expand to fully cover intersecting elements, and insertion positions are clamped away from element interiors. Word navigation uses `split_word_pieces` plus a custom separator set so paths and punctuation break into Vim/editor-friendly pieces.

Input dispatch splits between regular keymap-driven editing and Vim modal handling. Insert mode reuses the editor keymap; normal mode interprets motions, operators, text objects, line opening, paste/yank, and mode transitions while tracking pending operators in `vim_pending`. Rendering uses a lazily computed wrap cache and supports plain, masked, and highlight-overlay modes. `WidgetRef` and `StatefulWidgetRef` implementations render visible wrapped lines, color atomic elements cyan, and maintain scroll so the cursor stays on screen. The extensive tests cover Unicode, wrapping, keymap edge cases, Vim semantics, and randomized fuzz invariants.

#### Function details

##### `is_word_separator`  (lines 48–50)

```
fn is_word_separator(ch: char) -> bool
```

**Purpose**: Checks whether a character belongs to the custom punctuation set used to split words.

**Data flow**: Reads a `char`, tests membership in the `WORD_SEPARATORS` string, and returns `bool`.

**Call relations**: Word-piece splitting calls this repeatedly to distinguish separator runs from ordinary word runs.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (split_word_pieces).


##### `split_word_pieces`  (lines 52–76)

```
fn split_word_pieces(run: &str) -> Vec<(usize, &str)>
```

**Purpose**: Breaks a non-whitespace run into contiguous subpieces that alternate between separator and non-separator characters.

**Data flow**: Consumes `run: &str`, iterates Unicode word-boundary segments with `split_word_bound_indices`, then further subdivides each segment whenever `is_word_separator` changes state. It returns a `Vec<(usize, &str)>` of piece offsets relative to the original run.

**Call relations**: Backward and forward word navigation use this helper so punctuation-heavy text like paths or dotted identifiers behaves as multiple word pieces.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (is_word_separator); called by 2 (beginning_of_previous_word, end_of_next_word_from); 1 external calls (new).

**Call relations**: Called by `replace_range` for ordinary edits and by kill operations after they have already captured removed text.
